title: What is TypeScript and why use it over JavaScript?
answer: **TypeScript** is a typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JS. It adds static types, interfaces, generics, and enums — catching errors at compile time, improving editor autocomplete and refactoring, and making large codebases easier to maintain. All valid JS is valid TS.

title: What is the difference between interface and type?
answer: Both describe object shapes. **`interface`** is extendable and supports declaration merging — ideal for public/object APIs. **`type`** is more flexible: it can alias unions, intersections, primitives, tuples, and mapped types. Use `interface` for objects you may extend, `type` for unions and computed types.

title: What is the difference between any and unknown?
answer: **`any`** opts out of type checking entirely — anything goes. **`unknown`** is the type-safe counterpart: you can assign anything to it, but you must narrow it (via `typeof`, checks, or assertions) before using it. Prefer `unknown` to keep type safety.

title: What are generics and why are they useful?
answer: **Generics** let you write reusable code parameterized by type while preserving type information: `function identity<T>(x: T): T { return x; }`. They keep functions, classes, and types flexible yet type-safe — e.g. `Array<T>`, `Promise<T>` — instead of falling back to `any`.

title: What are union and intersection types?
answer: A **union** `A | B` allows a value to be one of several types; you narrow it before use. An **intersection** `A & B` combines types so the value has all members of both. Unions model 'either/or'; intersections model 'and'/composition.

title: What is type narrowing and what are type guards?
answer: **Narrowing** is how TS refines a broad type to a specific one within a branch using `typeof`, `instanceof`, `in`, or equality checks. A **user-defined type guard** is a function returning `x is Type`, telling the compiler the narrowed type when it returns true.

title: What are utility types like Partial, Pick, and Omit?
answer: Built-in generic helpers transform types: `Partial<T>` makes all props optional, `Required<T>` the reverse, `Pick<T,K>` selects keys, `Omit<T,K>` removes keys, `Record<K,V>` builds a map type, and `Readonly<T>` makes props immutable. They reduce repetitive type definitions.

title: What are enums and what are their trade-offs?
answer: **Enums** name a set of related constants (`enum Status { Active, Done }`). Numeric enums get reverse mappings and add runtime code; `const enum` inlines values. Many teams prefer **union of string literals** (`type Status = 'active' | 'done'`) for zero runtime cost and simpler typing.

title: What are mapped types?
answer: A **mapped type** builds a new type by iterating over the keys of another: `type Optional<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] }`. With key remapping and modifiers (`?`, `readonly`, `-`) they power utility types and let you transform shapes programmatically.

title: What are conditional types?
answer: A **conditional type** chooses a type based on a relationship: `T extends U ? X : Y`. Combined with `infer` it can extract types — e.g. `type ElementType<T> = T extends (infer E)[] ? E : T`. They enable highly generic, self-adjusting type utilities.

title: What do keyof and typeof do in TypeScript?
description: Explain the keyof and typeof type operators.
answer: **`keyof T`** produces a union of T's property names. The **type-level `typeof`** captures the type of a runtime value/object. Together they enable safe key access: `function get<T, K extends keyof T>(o: T, k: K): T[K]`.

title: How do you make values immutable in TypeScript?
answer: Use the **`readonly`** modifier on properties or `ReadonlyArray<T>`, and `Readonly<T>` for whole objects. **`as const`** freezes a literal to its narrowest readonly type (great for tuples and config). These are compile-time guarantees, not runtime freezes.

title: What is the never type?
answer: **`never`** represents values that never occur — functions that always throw or never return, and impossible branches. It's useful for **exhaustiveness checks**: in a `switch` over a union, assigning the default case to `never` makes the compiler error if you add a new variant and forget to handle it.

title: What is structural typing?
answer: TypeScript is **structurally typed**: compatibility is based on a type's shape, not its name. If an object has all the required members of a type, it's assignable to it — even without explicitly implementing it. This contrasts with nominal typing in languages like Java.

title: How does TypeScript handle null and undefined safely?
answer: With **`strictNullChecks`**, `null`/`undefined` aren't assignable to other types unless explicitly allowed (`string | null`). **Optional chaining** `a?.b` short-circuits on nullish values, and **nullish coalescing** `a ?? b` provides a fallback only for `null`/`undefined`.

title: What are type assertions and when should you use them?
answer: A **type assertion** (`value as Type`) tells the compiler to treat a value as a specific type when you know more than it does (e.g. DOM queries). They're compile-time only — no runtime conversion or check — so overuse can hide real bugs. Prefer narrowing where possible.

title: What are declaration files (.d.ts)?
answer: **`.d.ts`** files describe the types of existing JavaScript (libraries, globals) without implementation, so TS can type-check code that uses them. Many packages ship their own; others have community types under `@types/*` (DefinitelyTyped).

title: What does the 'strict' flag in tsconfig enable?
answer: **`strict: true`** turns on a family of checks — `strictNullChecks`, `noImplicitAny`, `strictFunctionTypes`, `strictBindCallApply`, `alwaysStrict`, and more. It maximizes type safety and is strongly recommended for new projects to catch the most bugs at compile time.

title: What are generic constraints?
answer: A **generic constraint** restricts a type parameter using `extends`, so you can safely access guaranteed members: `function longest<T extends { length: number }>(a: T, b: T)`. It keeps generics flexible while ensuring the type has what the function needs.

title: What is declaration merging?
answer: **Declaration merging** lets multiple declarations with the same name combine into one definition. Two `interface` declarations with the same name merge their members — useful for augmenting third-party types or extending global interfaces (e.g. adding a property to `Window`). `type` aliases cannot be merged this way.
